examples.title: Algorithm: Access Matrix Permission Check
examples.description: Demonstrates how to check if a domain has permission to perform an operation on an object using the Access Matrix model
examples.code:
ALGORITHM CheckAccess
INPUT: domain, object, operation, accessMatrix
OUTPUT: boolean (true if access granted, false otherwise)

BEGIN
 // accessMatrix is a 2D array where
 // rows represent domains, columns represent objects
 // Each cell contains a set of allowed operations
 
 // Find the row index for the given domain
 domainIndex ← FindDomainIndex(accessMatrix, domain)
 
 // Find the column index for the given object 
 objectIndex ← FindObjectIndex(accessMatrix, object)
 
 // Get the rights set for this domain-object pair
 rightsSet ← accessMatrix[domainIndex][objectIndex]
 
 // Check if the requested operation exists in rights set
 IF operation ∈ rightsSet THEN
 RETURN true
 ELSE
 RETURN false
 ENDIF
END

ALGORITHM GrantAccess
INPUT: domain, object, operation, accessMatrix
OUTPUT: updated accessMatrix

BEGIN
 domainIndex ← FindDomainIndex(accessMatrix, domain)
 objectIndex ← FindObjectIndex(accessMatrix, object)
 
 // Add the operation to the domain's rights for this object
 ADD operation TO accessMatrix[domainIndex][objectIndex]
 
 RETURN accessMatrix
END

ALGORITHM RevokeAccess
INPUT: domain, object, operation, accessMatrix
OUTPUT: updated accessMatrix

BEGIN
 domainIndex ← FindDomainIndex(accessMatrix, domain)
 objectIndex ← FindObjectIndex(accessMatrix, object)
 
 // Remove the operation from the domain's rights
 REMOVE operation FROM accessMatrix[domainIndex][objectIndex]
 
 RETURN accessMatrix
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the core operations of the Access Matrix model. The access matrix is a 2D structure where rows represent protection domains (users/processes), columns represent objects (files, devices), and each cell contains the set of operations allowed. The CheckAccess algorithm verifies if a domain has permission for a specific operation on an object. GrantAccess and RevokeAccess allow modification of permissions.

examples.title: Python: Access Control List (ACL) Implementation
examples.description: Implements ACL-based protection where each object stores its own access control list
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates Access Control Lists (ACLs), one of the four ways to implement the Access Matrix model. In ACLs, each object (column) stores a list of domains and their permitted operations. This approach makes it easy to determine who has access to a particular object and easy to revoke permissions. The implementation shows adding objects, granting/revoking access, and checking permissions.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for grant/revoke, O(n) for check where n is number of domains with access to object
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is total (domain, object) pairs with permissions

examples.title: Python: Capability Lists Implementation
examples.description: Implements capability-based protection where each domain holds capabilities (tickets) to objects
examples.explanation: This implementation demonstrates Capability Lists, another way to implement the Access Matrix. Here, each domain (row) maintains a list of capabilities it holds - each capability is like a ticket or key granting access to specific objects with specific rights. This approach makes it easy to determine all access rights of a given domain and supports capability transfer between processes. However, revocation is more difficult as capabilities may be distributed.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for grant/check, O(n) for revoke across all domains if needed
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is total (domain, object) pairs with capabilities

examples.title: C: Access Matrix Implementation with Permission Check
examples.description: Implements a basic access matrix in C with functions to check, grant, and revoke permissions
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates the Access Matrix model for OS protection. It creates a 2D matrix where rows represent protection domains (users) and columns represent objects (files, devices). Each cell contains boolean flags for different access rights (read, write, execute, print). The implementation includes functions to add domains and objects, grant permissions, and check access. The demonstration shows how the Principle of Least Privilege works - guests have minimal access while admins have more privileges.
examples.timeComplexity: O(d + o) for find operations where d=domains, o=objects; O(1) for access checks once indices are found
examples.spaceComplexity: O(d × o) for the full matrix storage
